name: Iwanaga-hime
description: Iwanaga-hime, often translated as 'Princess Long-Rock,' is a significant, though often overlooked, figure in Japanese mythology. She is a daughter of Ōyamatsumi, the god of mountains, and is primarily associated with rocks, stones, and, most importantly, longevity and permanence. Her importance is highlighted through her role in a pivotal myth involving Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu, who was sent to rule over the terrestrial realm. Iwanaga-hime represents the steadfastness and enduring nature of the earth, contrasting with her sister Konohana-Sakuya-hime, the blossom princess, who embodies ephemeral beauty and fleeting life. While Konohana-Sakuya-hime is often favored for her beauty, Iwanaga-hime's role is crucial for ensuring the stability and enduring legacy of the imperial line, highlighting the ancient Japanese reverence for the enduring power of nature. Despite her less glamorous associations, Iwanaga-hime is a powerful representation of the timeless aspects of the natural world.
subtitle: Princess Long-Rock

keyMyths.title: The Rejection of Iwanaga-hime
keyMyths.description: The most prominent myth involving Iwanaga-hime concerns Ninigi-no-Mikoto, who was offered both Iwanaga-hime and her beautiful sister, Konohana-Sakuya-hime, in marriage by their father, Ōyamatsumi. Ninigi, captivated by Konohana-Sakuya-hime's beauty, rejected Iwanaga-hime. This rejection angered Ōyamatsumi, who explained that had Ninigi accepted both sisters, the descendants of Ninigi would have possessed the enduring life of rocks (Iwanaga-hime) and the ephemeral beauty of blossoms (Konohana-Sakuya-hime). Because of this rejection, the imperial line, and all humans, are now mortal like the cherry blossoms. The source is the Kojiki.

keyMyths.title: Ōyamatsumi's Intentions
keyMyths.description: This myth emphasizes the intended balance between permanence and transience in the world. Ōyamatsumi's desire for Ninigi to accept both daughters reveals a deeper philosophical understanding of life's dual nature. The rejection of Iwanaga-hime disrupts this balance, leading to the introduction of mortality into the imperial line. It highlights the consequences of prioritizing fleeting beauty over enduring strength, a theme that resonates throughout Japanese folklore. The source is the Nihon Shoki.

keyMyths.title: Iwanaga-hime's Silent Acceptance
keyMyths.description: While not explicitly stated in many accounts, Iwanaga-hime's reaction to her rejection is often interpreted as one of silent acceptance. Her association with rocks and mountains suggests a stoic endurance in the face of adversity. She embodies the steadfastness of the earth, accepting her role even when unappreciated. This quiet strength makes her a compelling figure, representing the often-overlooked virtues of resilience and permanence. This interpretation is supported by scholarly analysis of Shinto symbolism and the roles of female deities.
